does: This paper introduces General Preference Reinforcement Learning (GPRL), a framework that addresses the limitation of scalar reward models in post-training large language models. Rather than collapsing multi-dimensional quality into a single scalar score, the method uses a General Preference Model (GPM) that embeds responses into k skew-symmetric subspaces to capture structured, intransitivity-aware preferences across multiple quality dimensions. GPRL propagates this k-dimensional structure through policy updates by computing per-dimension group-relative advantages, normalizing each dimension independently so that no single axis dominates the optimization. This allows the approach to simultaneously leverage online RL's continuous exploration benefits on open-ended tasks while maintaining the structured preference information that preference optimization methods rely on.
connects: This work bridges two previously disconnected post-training paradigms for LLM alignment: online reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (effective for math and code via programmatic verifiers) and preference optimization methods (handling open-ended generation). The paper responds to fundamental limitations in existing reward modeling approaches, particularly how scalar reward signals can lead to reward hacking or collapse onto single dimensions of quality. It builds on preference learning and Bradley-Terry-style comparison frameworks but extends them to handle the multi-dimensional nature of open-ended task quality and intransitive preferences. The work combines insights from structured reward learning with modern RL policy update mechanisms, directly addressing the gap between task-specific verifiable rewards and the continuous exploration that powers emergent reasoning in online RL.
enables: This framework enables more effective post-training of LLMs on open-ended tasks by maintaining multiple quality dimensions during policy optimization, preventing the pathological collapse seen with scalar rewards. GPRL opens pathways toward unified alignment procedures that combine online exploration with structured preference learning, potentially enabling emergent reasoning capabilities on subjective, hard-to-specify tasks beyond mathematics and code. The multi-dimensional preference representation could support more nuanced human feedback incorporation, where different quality axes (e.g., helpfulness, creativity, safety) are optimized according to their own scales. Future work could explore how to automatically discover or learn the optimal dimensionality k, how to incorporate human preference judgments that naturally express multi-dimensional trade-offs, and whether this structure generalizes across model scales and task domains.
